hero.title: Pebble Beach Beneath
hero.description: Plage d'Étretat sits on Normandy's Côte d'Albâtre, famed for its white chalk cliffs, natural arches ("Gates"), and the iconic "Needle" rock formation. Sunny days look like postcards, cloudy days feel like movies—but whatever the weather, treat the tides as your "first rule".

overview.title: What You'll See Here
overview.desc: From the "clack-clack" of pebbles to three natural stone arches, Étretat's coastline packages geology with literature, painting, and sea breeze.
overview.cliffs_desc: White chalk cliffs eroded by the sea form natural arches; the most famous are typically called Porte d'Aval / Porte d'Amont.
overview.pebble_desc: The beach is made of pebbles, making walking harder; but that's exactly why it's better to "slow down" and listen to the waves.
overview.tides_desc: At low tide you can explore the reefs and cave areas; but high tide may trap you between two sections of cliffs—always check the tide times.
overview.source_info: Information extracted from public sources (Normandy Tourism/Local Tourism Office and Wikipedia), combined with basic info from Google Maps.

photos.title: Photo Gallery
photos.desc: Few but refined images, with clear captions and navigation.
photos.alt1: Porte d'Aval and the reefs at low tide
photos.alt2: Pebble beach and white chalk cliffs

tips.title: How to Enjoy It More
tips.desc: Split your visit into 3 parts: Beach → Viewpoints → Back to town. The core rule: 'Check the tides, leave buffer time.'
tips.step1_title: First, find your rhythm at the beach
tips.step1_desc: Walk to the open viewing area, observe the wave line, wind direction, and crowd flow.
tips.step2_desc: If you plan to get close to the arches/reefs, you must check the tide schedule in advance.
tips.step3_title: Climb to cliff trails for panoramic views
tips.step3_desc: From above, the coastline's arc and light changes are most beautiful, especially at sunset.
tips.safety_desc: Walking toward the arches, high tide may block your return path. The safest approach: only enter during low tide windows, and leave at least 1 hour of buffer time for your return.
tips.safety_highlight: only enter during low tide windows
tips.rule_desc: Pebbles are the coastline's natural "shock absorbers" that reduce storm surge impact on embankments. Taking them is prohibited; skip stones instead of taking them home.

map.title: Location & Map
map.desc: Use the interactive map to explore Plage d'Étretat and plan your visit.
map.golden_hour: Parking Advice — Parking in Étretat is very limited. Use the Grand Val or train station parking lots outside the town center (15 min walk to the beach).
map.photo_spot: Photo Spot — The cliff trails on both sides (Amont and Aval) offer the best panoramic views. Low tide on the beach provides great close-ups of the arches.
map.route_plan: Route Planning — Click to plan your route in Google Maps. If driving, be aware that summer weekends can be extremely crowded.
map.parking_tip: Peak hours tip: Park outside the town center and walk in to save time searching for a spot.
map.open_hours_desc: Public waterfront; lifeguards may be present in summer. Check local notices on arrival.
map.best_light_desc: Sunset hour paints the limestone cliffs in warm amber tones. Arrive 1 hour before sunset.

sources.title: Data & References
sources.desc: To keep information credible, we only cite publicly accessible pages.

sources.item3_desc: Geomorphological structure and background info on the "Gates/Needle"

reviews.title: What Visitors Say
reviews.desc: Real reviews from Google Maps travelers who have experienced Plage d'Étretat.
reviews.disclaimer: Rating and review data sourced from Google Maps (last updated: 2026). We display a selection of verified high-rated reviews. For the complete and latest reviews, click the link below.
reviews.reviewer1_content: Absolutely the most spectacular coastline in Normandy. The white chalk cliffs (Porte d'Aval) and the Needle (L'Aiguille) are breathtaking. Make sure to wear suitable shoes for walking on the pebbles, and definitely explore the hidden caves at low tide.
reviews.reviewer2_content: The view at sunset is unbeatable! When the sun paints the white cliffs golden, the entire beach looks like an oil painting. Highly recommend climbing the Amont cliff trail for a panoramic view of the entire Étretat bay curve.
reviews.reviewer3_content: The pebble beach is very unique, and the 'clack-clack' sound of the stones rolling as the waves recede is very therapeutic. It gets quite windy here, so bring a light jacket even in summer. Parking can be difficult, best to arrive early or park on the outskirts.
reviews.reviewer4_content: Every visit feels different. You MUST pay attention to the tide times! We almost got trapped in the reef area once due to the rising tide. As long as you are prepared, the natural scenery here will never disappoint.
reviews.reviewer5_content: Magnificent natural landscape. While walking on the pebbles takes some effort, seeing those massive natural stone arches up close makes it all worthwhile. The beach is clean and it's the perfect place to experience the northern French coast.
